Document Title: Act of 20 July 2017 - Water Law (Consolidated text)
Family Title: Act of 20 July 2017 - Water Law
Family Summary: The Polish Water Law establishes an integrated framework for managing water resources with strong relevance for climate adaptation, energy security, and disaster risk management. It explicitly addresses climate-related pressures by prioritising protection against floods and droughts, introducing risk-based planning, flood hazard and risk mapping, and the use of retention, polders, and coastal protection infrastructure to reduce climate-driven extremes. The Act supports the energy sector, particularly hydropower and water-dependent infrastructure, by regulating water use, permitting, and the operation of water and flood-control structures while balancing these activities with ecological objectives. Through river-basin planning, hydrological and meteorological monitoring, and coordination with EU climate and environmental directives, the law strengthens Poland’s resilience to climate change, reduces vulnerability to water-related disasters, and ensures that energy development and water management contribute to long-term environmental and societal safety. The last amendment provided by the Act (Dz.U. 2025 poz. 1535) modernises and expands Poland’s legal framework for offshore wind and other renewables.

Document Title: Biofuels Act - No. 1676 of 30/12/2024 (latest amendment)
Family Title: Act on Sustainable Biofuels and on the Reduction of Greenhouse Gases (Biofuels Act)
Family Summary:
The purpose of the Act is to promote the use of sustainable biofuels for land transport and to reduce cradle-to-grave greenhouse gas emissions from transport in order to contribute to the fulfillment of Denmark's international climate commitments. The Act sets mandatory levels of biofuels use for companies and is regularly amended to update these levels.The 2016 amendment introduces requirements for the use of 0.9 per cent of advanced biofuels in land transport fuels, amends the definition of which companies are covered by the law, and authorises to establish rules for compliance with MRV regulation to promote CO2 emissions reduction from maritime transport and rules on access to ships. 2019 and 2020 updates are to the list of advanced biofuels; and duty of companies to ensure biofuels make up at least 7.6% of company’s total annual sale of fuels (following European counting rules).

The latest amendment of 30 December 2024 expands coverage of the Act to the aviation sector and stipulates that the Minister may lay down rules on penalties for fines for violations of the ReFuelEU Aviation Regulation. The Minister for Climate, Energy and Utilities may also lay down rules on payments, including fees or other payments, to cover the authorities' costs of administration and supervision of compliance with the ReFuelEU Aviation Regulation and the FuelEU Maritime Regulation and of rules issued pursuant to this Act, the ReFuelEU Aviation Regulation and the FuelEU Maritime Regulation.

Document Title: Law relating to the coordination of the federal sustainable development policy
Family Title: Law relating to the coordination of the federal sustainable development policy
Family Summary: The latest version of the Act on Co-ordination of Federal Policy on Sustainable Development calls for the development of a long-term vision for Sustainable Development that would include goals for 2050 and be based on interdepartmental and vertical co-operation among between the federal level and federated entities through the specifically created Interdepartmental Commission on Sustainable Development.

Document Title: Law amending the law of 29 April 1999 on the organization of the electricity market
Family Title: Act on Organization of the Electricity Market, amended by law of 12-may-19
Family Summary: The Act regulates organisation of the electricity market and contributes to meeting international commitments related to climate change. This is to be done in particular through promotion of renewable electricity generation, among others by providing federal feed-in tariffs, and organising a federal green certificate scheme. It defines the extensive competences of CREG (the Commission for Regulation of Electricity and Gas) in fixing transport and distribution tariffs, as well as regulating the prices of energy to prevent adverse price increases. The Act further guarantees connection of renewable electricity sources to the grid. It also supports the promotion of renewable energies in planning for electricity sources choice, lays down provisions for feed-in tariffs for renewable electricity production not addressed by specific regional regulation, mandates the federal Minister for Energy to stimulate tenders for renewable electricity generation installations and especially wind power, provides for adoption of specific regulation organizing the green certification scheme. The federal green certificate scheme is further detailed in the Royal order on the creation of a mechanism to support electricity production from renewable energy sources of 2012 (modifying previous regulation from 2002). The Order requires electricity transmission system operators to purchase green or CHP energy certificates at a guaranteed minimum price. Renewable energy facilities (offshore wind farms, PV facilities commissioned before 01/08/2012, hydro or tidal energy facilities and geothermal energy generation facilities) are entitled to receive this support for 10 years after the facility is commissioned. The Act was amended in 2019 by the law on the organisation of the electricity market with a view to introducing a competitive tendering procedure for the construction and operation of production facilities in the marine areas under the jurisdiction of Belgium and ratifying the Royal Decree of 11 February 2019, amending the Royal Decree of 16 July 2002 on the establishment of mechanisms to promote electricity produced from renewable energy sources.

Document Title: Act of 17 December 2020 on the promotion of electricity production in marine wind farms (consolidated text)
Family Title: Act of 17 December 2020 on the promotion of electricity production in marine wind farms, last amended by Act of 9 October 2025 (Offshore Wind Act)
Family Summary: The Act of 17 December 2020 on promoting electricity generation in offshore wind farms aims to boost the production of renewable energy from wind in the Baltic sea, in accordance with EU regulations.The Amendment brought by the Act of 9 October 2025 aims to "unblock" the second phase of Polish offshore development. By removing rigid administrative bottlenecks (like the 50m placement rule) and adding the intervention auction fail-safe, the government aimed to ensure that Poland meets its target of 5.9 GW by 2030 and 18 GW by 2040.

Document Title: Act of 21 November 2024 amending Act of 11 January 2018 on electromobility and alternative fuels
Family Title: Act of 11 January 2018 on electromobility and alternative fuels, last amended by Act of 21 November 2024
Family Summary:
The Act of 11 January 2018 on Electromobility and Alternative Fuels sets out Poland’s legal framework for the development and operation of infrastructure for alternative fuels in transport — including electric charging stations and other low‑emission vehicle fuels — by defining technical requirements, obligations for public authorities to develop this infrastructure, information duties, the conditions for clean transport zones in municipalities, and the national policy framework for alternative fuel infrastructure, in order to implement key EU directives on clean transport and energy efficiency.

The Act of 21 November 2024 (Poz. 1853) amended this law to update and strengthen electromobility provisions, including: raising and clarifying targets for the share of electric vehicles in the fleets of central and local public bodies (e.g., requiring at least 22 % electric vehicles in relevant government and municipal fleets), restructuring or repealing certain older requirements, and introducing new obligations for municipalities to deploy zero‑emission or biomethane‑powered buses in public transport, as well as amendments to the rules for establishing clean transport zones (including automatic creation where air pollution thresholds are exceeded). It also updated annual reporting duties on fleet electrification

Document Title: Amendment Act of 27 November 2024
Family Title: Act of 20 February 2015 on renewable energy sources, last amended by Act of 9 October 2025 (RES Act)
Family Summary:
This Act defines the new regime for support to renewable energy sources (including wind energy, energy radiation of the sun, solar, aerothermal, geothermal, hydrothermal energy, hydropower, waves, currents error and tidal energy obtained from biomass, biogas, agricultural biogas and bioliquid - art. 2.22), aiming to stabilise the long-term support system, with the goal to prevent electricity prices form increasing in an uncontrolled manner. The Act also transposes the Directive 2009/28/EC on renewable energy into Polish legislation and aims to contribute to Poland's target of reaching 15% share of renewable energy production by 2020. The Act will provide continuing support to most existing installations (in operation by or before 31 December 2015) through certificates of origin, while future investments will compete for support through an auction system organised by the President of the Energy Regulatory Office (ERO). The auctions, applying to new installations over 10 kW of installed capacity, will be based on a type of contract-for-difference (CfD) granted, with separate auctions for old and new plants and for plants of 1 MW or less, as well as varied reference prices for the eighteen renewable sources reference groups. The chosen renewable plants will have to start generation within 24 months (solar plants), 72 months (wind plants) or 48 months (others), and they will be required to deliver at least 85% of the volume offered in the bid, under threat of penalty. The energy auctioned will be bought by the newly formed state-owned entity Operator Rozlicze? Energii Odnawialnej (for energy produced in installations above 500kW) or by electricity distribution companies (for energy produced in installations up to 500kW). Most existing installations will also be eligible to change to the auction support instrument from the current certificates of origin, except for hydropower plants over 5 MW, non-qualified biomass co-firing installations (art. 44) and biomass installations exceeding 50 MW of electrical power (or 150 MW of CHP power). The existing installations will continue to be eligible for the existing system of support through certificates of origin (introduced through the 1997 Energy Law), but a number of limits will apply:

  • support for max 15 years from the start of renewable energy production in the given installation;
  • support for hydropower of max 5 MW of capacity;
  • support for biomass co-firing installations of less than 150 MW capacity capped to equivalent of the average generation over 2011-2013;
  • large consumers (over 100 GWh of electricity consumed annually) and commodity brokerage houses are required to obtain certificates of origin for 14% and 15% of their annual electricity consumption for 2015 and 2016 respectively or pay the substitution fee. From 2017 the share is min 20%, but can be reduced by the Minister of Economy, if necessary due to specific market conditions. A unit substitution fee is set to a constant 300.03 PLN/MWh (2014 level, approx. 72 €/MWh), which should stabilise the price of certificates, as purchasing and redeeming of certificates of origin will only be allowed if the average market price of certificates remains below 75% of the substitution fee.

The Act also introduces a feed-in tariff system for micro installations of up to 10 kW (max 40 kW under special conditions - art. 41.7), while guaranteeing purchase by electricity distribution companies at 100% of market price for the next 15 years, until no later than December 31, 2035 (art. 41.5). The support also covers the so-called ‘prosumers,' who both produce and consume their own electricity. The subsidies amount to PLN 0.75 (EUR 0.18) per kWh to prosumers with a capacity of up to 3 kW (hydropower, wind, solar), and PLN 0.40-0.70 (EUR 0.10-0.17) for those with a capacity between 3 and 10 kW (biogas, hydropower, wind, solar). Moreover, there are no licensing/concession requirements for those micro generators that do not have active business. The Act was amended in June 2018, alongside the 2016 Act on investments in wind power and the 1994 Construction Act, in order to boost offshore wind investment. It was further amended in August 2019 to extend the agreements on grid connection and updates the auction mechanism.The Act was notably amended by bill 1129/2021 to extend the period for auctions of energy sales, set auction volumes, and remove obligations for small producers of renewable energy. The Amendments from November 2024 and October 2025 to the Renewable Energy Sources Act represent a strategic pivot toward "energy democratization," fundamentally increasing the profitability and accessibility of green energy. The November 2024 Act introduced a more favorable "net-billing" calculation and cut administrative wait times for solar building permits and grid connections from months to just 30 to 45 days. The October 2025 Act expanded these rights to urban dwellers by allowing energy cooperatives to operate in cities and granting "tenant prosumers" the flexibility to install solar panels on carports and garages rather than just roofs. Together, these laws establish renewable energy as an "overriding public interest".

Document Title: Amending Act of 21 November 2025
Family Title: Act of 12 of June 2015 on on the greenhouse gas emission allowance trading scheme, last amended by Act of 21 November 2025
Family Summary: The Act of 12 June 2015 on the greenhouse gas emission allowance trading scheme establishes the Polish legal framework for implementing the EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS). The current text regulates which installations and aviation activities are covered, the rules for allocation, auctioning and trading of emission allowances, monitoring, reporting and verification of emissions, operation of registry accounts, and compliance obligations, including penalties for non-compliance. The latest amendment of 2025 (n.1863) mainly introduces financial and budgetary adjustments rather than changing the substance of the ETS.

Document Title: Act 3 of October 2008 (EIA Act) - Initial text
Family Title: Act 3 of October 2008 (EIA Act)
Family Summary: The EIA Act establishes Poland’s legal framework for access to environmental information, public participation in environmental protection and environmental impact assessment (EIA) procedures. It guarantees that environmental information (e.g., data on pollution, plans and decisions) is made available to the public and sets out how citizens and organisations can participate in environmental decision‑making. It also implements EU and international law on environmental impact assessments by defining the scope and procedures for assessing the likely environmental effects of proposed projects and plansLastly, the act states that the EIA report shall include a description of the likely significant effects of the project on the air, water, soil, climate, fauna, flora, landscape and cultural heritage, including transboundary effects and estimate of emissions, including greenhouse gases, and a description of the measures planned to prevent, reduce or offset adverse environmental impacts.

Document Title: Act of 1 December 2022 amending the Act on Excise Duty and certain other acts
Family Title: Act of 1 December 2022 amending the Act on Excise Duty and certain other acts
Family Summary:
The existing excise and income tax preferences for zero- and low-emission vehicles provide an instrument to support the development of electromobility and to encourage public interest in electromobility. The excise tax exemption applies to electric, hydrogen (indefinite) and plug-in hybrid vehicles with an internal combustion engine capacity not exceeding 2000 cm³ (timely exemption).

Currently, pursuant to the Act of 1 December 2022 amending the Act of 06 December 2008 on Excise Duty and certain other acts (Journal of Laws item 2707), the excise duty exemption previously in force until 31 December 2022 for passenger cars that are plug-in hybrid vehicles with an internal combustion engine capacity not exceeding 2000 cm³ has been extended until 31 December 2029. For other hybrid vehicles with an engine capacity of up to 3500 cm³, halved excise duty rates apply from 1 January 2020, varying according to the type of hybrid and the capacity of the internal combustion engine.

Document Title: Act of 14 December 2018 on the promotion of electricity from high-efficiency cogeneration (the CHP Act) - Consolidated text
Family Title: Act of 14 December 2018 on the promotion of electricity from high-efficiency cogeneration, last amended by Act of 27 November 2024 (CHP Act)
Family Summary:
This act is centred around the promotion of electricity from high-efficiency cogeneration through a support scheme in the form of cogeneration bonus (CHP auction) for electricity generators from high-efficiency cogeneration.

This subsidy scheme is approved by the Polish Ministry of Climate and Environment, the Consultative Council of the Modernization Fund and the European Investment Bank. It totals an allocation of PLN 1.5 Billion aimed at co/financing the development of cogeneration with municipal biogas in the form of grant or loans covering 50% or 100% of eligible costs respectively. It supports the construction of new installations, or the extension or modernization of existing installations, for the fermentation of municipal organic waste and the use of the obtained biogas to generate energy through high-efficiency cogeneration.

Applications from companies are open from February to June in 2024, and are covering costs from September 2022 up to December 2030.The Act of 27 November 2024 updated provisions relevant to cogeneration in the context of industrial customer eligibility for renewables‑related entitlements and compliance obligations — notably by aligning the conditions for industrial consumers to benefit from exemptions or support under the cogeneration law with new renewable energy criteria, such as demonstrating reductions in greenhouse gas emissions or improvements in energy efficiency, and requiring updated attestations and documentation for eligibility in 2025. These changes reflect the integration of high‑efficiency cogeneration support with broader renewable energy and energy‑intensive consumer rules established in the amended Renewable Energy Sources Act.

Document Title: Act of 20 May 2016 on investments in the field of wind farms, last amended by Act of 9 March 2023 (consolidated text)
Family Title: Act of 20 May 2016 on investments in wind farms, last amended by Act of 9 March 2023
Family Summary: The Act of 20 May 2016 on investments in the field of wind power plants sets out special rules for the planning, construction and location of wind farms in Poland, including spatial planning requirements and conditions for siting turbine installations. Over time this Act has been amended to liberalise wind energy development — most notably by the Act of 9 March 2023, which relaxed the stringent distance rule that previously prohibited turbines within ten times their height from residential buildings by allowing siting under local spatial plans with a minimum distance of 700 meters instead of the 10H rule, creating more opportunities for onshore wind projects.

Family Title: Act of 8 December 2017 on the capacity market, last amended by Act of 12 September 2025
Family Summary: TThe Act on the Capacity Market’s purpose is to ensure medium- and long-term security of electricity supply to end users in a manner that is cost-effective, non-discriminatory, and consistent with the principles of sustainable development.; in 2025, this framework was amended to introduce a new mechanism of supplementary (uzupełniające) capacity auctions that can be conducted after the main auctions if they fail to secure adequate capacity for future delivery periods, allowing additional contracting of capacity and helping address potential shortages under evolving market conditions.

Document Title: Act of 15 May 2015 on substances that deplete the ozone layer and on certain fluorinated greenhouse gases, last amended by Act of 21 November 2025 (Consolidated text)
Family Title: Act of 15 May 2015 on substances that deplete the ozone layer and on certain fluorinated greenhouse gases, last amended by Act of 21 November 2025
Family Summary: The Act of 15 May 2015 establishes Poland’s national legal framework for implementing EU and international regulations on ozone-depleting substances (ODS) and fluorinated greenhouse gases (F-gases), ensuring compliance with key EU instruments such as Regulation (UE) No 517/2014 on F-gases and Regulation (WE) No 1005/2009 on ODS.The Act also defines certification requirements for personnel and businesses handling such substances, establishes control and enforcement powers for authorities, and provides for administrative sanctions for non-compliance. The 2025 amendment to the Act of 15 May 2015 primarily updates Poland’s legal framework to align with the revised EU regulations on fluorinated greenhouse gases and ozone-depleting substances, ensuring continued compliance with strengthened EU phase-down, control, and reporting requirements. It also adjusts administrative and financial provisions.

Document Title: Act of 21 February 2025 amending  Act of 25 August 2006 on biocomponents and liquid biofuels
Family Title: Act of 25 August 2006 on biocomponents and liquid biofuels, last amended by Act of 21 February 2025
Family Summary: This Act (last amended in January 2015) establishes that the producers, importers and suppliers of fuels are obliged to meet an annual quota of biofuels in the total amount of liquid fuels produced, supplied and imported. The obligation levels are determined every three years for a period of six years by the Council of Ministers. The following biocomponents are eligible: bioethanol, biomethanol, biobutanol, ester dimethylether, pure vegetable oil, bio liquid hydrocarbons, bio propane-butane, liquefied biomethane, compressed biomethane and biohydrogen, which are produced from biomass for use in the manufacture of liquid fuels or biofuels (art. 2 par. 1 no. 3). The biocomponents and bioliguids can only count towards the quota and be eligible for financial support, if they meet the sustainability criteria set out within the recognized certification system (art. 28b-28c) approved by the European Commission, including GHG emission reduction, protection of land with high biodiversity value, peatland protection and sustainable farming criteria (art. 28a).The Act is detailed by Regulation of 23 July 2013 on the National Indicative Targets for 2013-2018). The Regulation established that the obliged companies have to ensure that biofuels make up the following quotas of the company's total annual sale or consumption of fuel: 7.10% between 2013 and 2016, 7.80% in 2017 and 8.50% in 2018.The Act of 21 February 2025 amended Poland’s Act on Biocomponents and Liquid Biofuels to transpose key elements of the EU Renewable Energy Directive (RED II) into national law and modernize the framework for renewable fuels used in transport, including: setting new levels of the National Indicative Target (NCW) for renewable energy content in transport fuels from 2025 through 2030 (with incremental target percentages); expanding the list of fuels eligible to meet the NCW to include advanced biocomponents (produced from waste or residues), biomethane, recycled liquid fuels, and renewable electricity; introducing updated definitions for biomass and biocomponents; and adjusting the registers, reporting duties, and oversight mechanisms for fuel suppliers — all to support increased use of renewable and low‑emission fuels and align Polish law with EU renewable energy requirements.

Document Title: Amending Act of 21 November 2025
Family Title: Act of 17 July 2009 on the system for managing emissions of greenhouse gases and other substances,  last amended by Act of 21 November 2025
Family Summary:
This Act introduces the legal basis for the management of national GHG emissions and other substances in order to fulfill Poland's obligations towards the EU and the UNFCCC. It allows pollution to be cut in the cheapest possible way and it introduces into Polish law the three mechanisms under the Kyoto Protocol: Emissions Trading, the Clean Development Mechanism and Joint Implementation This Act sets forth the following: - Responsibilities of the National Centre for Emission Balancing and Management; - The principles of the operation of the National System for Emission Balancing and Forecasting; - The principles of the management of emissions of GHGs and other substances; - The principles of the operation of the National Registry of the Kyoto Units and emission allowance; - The principles of trading in and managing the Kyoto units; - The principles of the operation of the National Green Investment Scheme and the Climate Account; - The terms and conditions of the management of the Joint Implementation projects in the territory of the Republic of Poland; and - The terms and conditions of the management outside the territory of the Republic of Poland for the Joint Implementation projects, and the Clean Development Mechanism projects. Projects implemented in following areas can be considered for implementation under the GIS Act: (i) energy efficiency; (ii) clean coal technologies; (iii) replacement of high-emission by low-emission fuel; (iv) avoidance or reduction of GHG emissions from the transport sector; (v) use of renewable energy sources; (vi) avoidance or reduction of methane emissions through recycling and use in power generation; (vii) GHG sequestration; (viii) actions to reduce or avoid GHG emissions, to absorb CO2 and adapt to climate change; (ix) research and development of renewable energy sources and advanced and innovative environmentally-friendly technologies; (x) educational activities.

The 2025 amendment (Dz.U. 2025 poz. 1863) to the Act of 17 July 2009 on the System to Manage the Emissions of Greenhouse Gases and Other Substances introduces several updates mainly to align Polish law with EU carbon-border adjustment mechanism (CBAM) and related EU requirements, and to strengthen administrative cooperation and enforcement: the amendment adds new EU legal references (including an EU Commission implementing decision) in the Act’s reference section and updates the scope of tasks of environmental and customs authorities so that they support implementation of CBAM reporting.

Document Title: Act of 20 February 2015 on renewable energy sources, last amended by Act of 9 October 2025 (Consolidated text)
Family Title: Act of 20 February 2015 on renewable energy sources, last amended by Act of 9 October 2025 (RES Act)
Family Summary:
This Act defines the new regime for support to renewable energy sources (including wind energy, energy radiation of the sun, solar, aerothermal, geothermal, hydrothermal energy, hydropower, waves, currents error and tidal energy obtained from biomass, biogas, agricultural biogas and bioliquid - art. 2.22), aiming to stabilise the long-term support system, with the goal to prevent electricity prices form increasing in an uncontrolled manner. The Act also transposes the Directive 2009/28/EC on renewable energy into Polish legislation and aims to contribute to Poland's target of reaching 15% share of renewable energy production by 2020. The Act will provide continuing support to most existing installations (in operation by or before 31 December 2015) through certificates of origin, while future investments will compete for support through an auction system organised by the President of the Energy Regulatory Office (ERO). The auctions, applying to new installations over 10 kW of installed capacity, will be based on a type of contract-for-difference (CfD) granted, with separate auctions for old and new plants and for plants of 1 MW or less, as well as varied reference prices for the eighteen renewable sources reference groups. The chosen renewable plants will have to start generation within 24 months (solar plants), 72 months (wind plants) or 48 months (others), and they will be required to deliver at least 85% of the volume offered in the bid, under threat of penalty. The energy auctioned will be bought by the newly formed state-owned entity Operator Rozlicze? Energii Odnawialnej (for energy produced in installations above 500kW) or by electricity distribution companies (for energy produced in installations up to 500kW). Most existing installations will also be eligible to change to the auction support instrument from the current certificates of origin, except for hydropower plants over 5 MW, non-qualified biomass co-firing installations (art. 44) and biomass installations exceeding 50 MW of electrical power (or 150 MW of CHP power). The existing installations will continue to be eligible for the existing system of support through certificates of origin (introduced through the 1997 Energy Law), but a number of limits will apply:

  • support for max 15 years from the start of renewable energy production in the given installation;
  • support for hydropower of max 5 MW of capacity;
  • support for biomass co-firing installations of less than 150 MW capacity capped to equivalent of the average generation over 2011-2013;
  • large consumers (over 100 GWh of electricity consumed annually) and commodity brokerage houses are required to obtain certificates of origin for 14% and 15% of their annual electricity consumption for 2015 and 2016 respectively or pay the substitution fee. From 2017 the share is min 20%, but can be reduced by the Minister of Economy, if necessary due to specific market conditions. A unit substitution fee is set to a constant 300.03 PLN/MWh (2014 level, approx. 72 €/MWh), which should stabilise the price of certificates, as purchasing and redeeming of certificates of origin will only be allowed if the average market price of certificates remains below 75% of the substitution fee.

The Act also introduces a feed-in tariff system for micro installations of up to 10 kW (max 40 kW under special conditions - art. 41.7), while guaranteeing purchase by electricity distribution companies at 100% of market price for the next 15 years, until no later than December 31, 2035 (art. 41.5). The support also covers the so-called ‘prosumers,' who both produce and consume their own electricity. The subsidies amount to PLN 0.75 (EUR 0.18) per kWh to prosumers with a capacity of up to 3 kW (hydropower, wind, solar), and PLN 0.40-0.70 (EUR 0.10-0.17) for those with a capacity between 3 and 10 kW (biogas, hydropower, wind, solar). Moreover, there are no licensing/concession requirements for those micro generators that do not have active business. The Act was amended in June 2018, alongside the 2016 Act on investments in wind power and the 1994 Construction Act, in order to boost offshore wind investment. It was further amended in August 2019 to extend the agreements on grid connection and updates the auction mechanism.The Act was notably amended by bill 1129/2021 to extend the period for auctions of energy sales, set auction volumes, and remove obligations for small producers of renewable energy. The Amendments from November 2024 and October 2025 to the Renewable Energy Sources Act represent a strategic pivot toward "energy democratization," fundamentally increasing the profitability and accessibility of green energy. The November 2024 Act introduced a more favorable "net-billing" calculation and cut administrative wait times for solar building permits and grid connections from months to just 30 to 45 days. The October 2025 Act expanded these rights to urban dwellers by allowing energy cooperatives to operate in cities and granting "tenant prosumers" the flexibility to install solar panels on carports and garages rather than just roofs. Together, these laws establish renewable energy as an "overriding public interest".

Document Title: Act of 27 April 2001 – Environmental Protection Law (Consolidated text)
Family Title: Act of 27 April 2001 – Environmental Protection Law
Family Summary:
The Environmental Protection Law (Prawo ochrony środowiska) of 27 April 2001 is Poland’s core statute regulating environmental protection. It sets out the principles, objectives and basic obligations for safeguarding the environment, including protecting air, water, soil, plants and animals, preventing and limiting pollution, ensuring sustainable use of natural resources, and establishing the framework for environmental decision‑making and permits (such as integrated environmental permits for industrial installations, emissions control, and ecological reviews). The law also defines duties for individuals and businesses using the environment, obligations for public authorities, economic instruments like fees and penalties, and enforcement mechanisms to ensure compliance with environmental standards. Its provisions help implement both national policy and EU environmental directives.

The Act of 7 November 2025 amending the Environmental Protection Law and certain other acts updated this framework by extending the duration of certain transitional provisions until 31 December 2026.

Document Title: Act of 25 August 2006 on biocomponents and liquid biofuels, last amended by Act of 21 February 2025 (Consolidated text)
Family Title: Act of 25 August 2006 on biocomponents and liquid biofuels, last amended by Act of 21 February 2025
Family Summary: This Act (last amended in January 2015) establishes that the producers, importers and suppliers of fuels are obliged to meet an annual quota of biofuels in the total amount of liquid fuels produced, supplied and imported. The obligation levels are determined every three years for a period of six years by the Council of Ministers. The following biocomponents are eligible: bioethanol, biomethanol, biobutanol, ester dimethylether, pure vegetable oil, bio liquid hydrocarbons, bio propane-butane, liquefied biomethane, compressed biomethane and biohydrogen, which are produced from biomass for use in the manufacture of liquid fuels or biofuels (art. 2 par. 1 no. 3). The biocomponents and bioliguids can only count towards the quota and be eligible for financial support, if they meet the sustainability criteria set out within the recognized certification system (art. 28b-28c) approved by the European Commission, including GHG emission reduction, protection of land with high biodiversity value, peatland protection and sustainable farming criteria (art. 28a).The Act is detailed by Regulation of 23 July 2013 on the National Indicative Targets for 2013-2018). The Regulation established that the obliged companies have to ensure that biofuels make up the following quotas of the company's total annual sale or consumption of fuel: 7.10% between 2013 and 2016, 7.80% in 2017 and 8.50% in 2018.The Act of 21 February 2025 amended Poland’s Act on Biocomponents and Liquid Biofuels to transpose key elements of the EU Renewable Energy Directive (RED II) into national law and modernize the framework for renewable fuels used in transport, including: setting new levels of the National Indicative Target (NCW) for renewable energy content in transport fuels from 2025 through 2030 (with incremental target percentages); expanding the list of fuels eligible to meet the NCW to include advanced biocomponents (produced from waste or residues), biomethane, recycled liquid fuels, and renewable electricity; introducing updated definitions for biomass and biocomponents; and adjusting the registers, reporting duties, and oversight mechanisms for fuel suppliers — all to support increased use of renewable and low‑emission fuels and align Polish law with EU renewable energy requirements.

Document Title: Amending Act of 9 October 2025
Family Title: Act of 10 April 1997- Energy Law
Family Summary: This law (with later amendments, lastly in September 2015) defines the principles of state energy policy, conditions for the supply and use of fuels and energy, and the framework for activities of energy companies. It also specifies the authorities responsible for the management of fuels and energy. Among others, it established the basis for independent electricity and gas production and access of independent power producers to the grid, principles for least cost and integrated resource planning, support to high efficiency heat and power production, demand side management and energy efficiency labels. It facilities the production of electricity from renewable energy sources (up to 5MW), making them eligible to benefit from a reduced grid connection fee and exempt from paying annual licence fees. Energy providers are allowed to incorporate costs of developing renewable energy into their tariff regimes. Since 1 October 2005, energy enterprises that generate, trade or purchase electricity are obliged to purchase or generate a certain amount of electricity from renewable sources of energy. The obligation will be met through the acquisition and redemption of certificates of origin that certify electricity produced from renewable sources. Transmissions and distribution operators are obliged to accept renewable sources of energy. Similar scheme for heat produced from renewable energy sources entered into force on 1 January 2007. The amendment of 26 July of 2013 simplified the setting up a micro-installation (renewable energy sources with no more than 40kW of total installed electrical capacity, connected to electrical grid of a voltage lower than 110kV or no more than 120 kW of total installed heat capacity). Applications to connect a micro-installation to the grid will no longer be subject to a connection fee nor required to attach documents confirming the admissibility of locating the installation in the investment area. In some cases, there will be no need for an interconnection agreement. It also expands the definition of renewable energy sources to aerothermal and hydrothermal, it provides the protection of vulnerable consumers, as well as support for energy-intensive industries. In order to make the gas market more transparent and competitive, the Small Tri-Pack imposes the obligation to sell at least 30% (40% since 2014 and 55% since 2015) of the gas at the exchange markets. The Act also exempts people producing energy from renewable resources for their own purposes from the obligation to obtain the license for the power supply. The amendment to the Energy Law from 26 September 2015 introduces measures necessary for the application of the 'REMIT Regulation' of the European Parliament and the Council Regulation no. 1227/2011 (25 October 2011) on the wholesale energy market integrity and transparency. It establishes a legal framework necessary for monitoring the practices of wholesale energy market participants, as well as detecting and deterring fraudulent practices in this sector.The "meter Act" of June 2021 amends this document and others to boost the deployment of smart meters in the country.In 2025, the Polish Energy Law was modified by multiple amendments to strengthen legal clarity, market functioning and support for the energy transition: the Act of 5 August 2025 updated the Energy Law itself and related statutes to address structural issues arising from corporate reorganisations (ensuring that obligations of reserve sellers and default sellers automatically transfer to successor companies after a corporate division), clarified how supplier duties carry over after such a division, and aligned transitional rules for obligated sellers in electricity and gas markets with ongoing company changes; this reform aimed to close legal gaps around entity succession and ensure continuity of supply and regulatory obligations following company splits. At the same time, the October amendment to the Offshore Wind Promotion Act also amended the Energy Law (among other statutes) to incorporate provisions facilitating renewable energy development, such as improving the grid connection regime for offshore wind farms, allowing shared use of offshore electrical infrastructure. These amendments both tighten control over conventional energy markets and simultaneously create a more enabling legal framework for renewable energy development, supporting Poland’s energy security and decarbonisation goals.

The Regulation established that the obliged companies have to ensure that biofuels make up the following quotas of the company's total annual sale or consumption of fuel: 7.10% between 2013 and 2016, 7.80% in 2017 and 8.50% in 2018.The Act of 21 February 2025 amended Poland’s Act on Biocomponents and Liquid Biofuels to transpose key elements of the EU Renewable Energy Directive (RED II) into national law and modernize the framework for renewable fuels used in transport, including: setting new levels of the National Indicative Target (NCW) for renewable energy content in transport fuels from 2025 through 2030 (with incremental target percentages); expanding the list of fuels eligible to meet the NCW to include advanced biocomponents (produced from waste or residues), biomethane, recycled liquid fuels, and renewable electricity; introducing updated definitions for biomass and biocomponents; and adjusting the registers, reporting duties, and oversight mechanisms for fuel suppliers — all to support increased use of renewable and low‑emission fuels and align Polish law with EU renewable energy requirements.

Document Title: Act of 17 July 2009 on the system for managing emissions of greenhouse gases and other substances (Initial text)
Family Title: Act of 17 July 2009 on the system for managing emissions of greenhouse gases and other substances,  last amended by Act of 21 November 2025
Family Summary:
This Act introduces the legal basis for the management of national GHG emissions and other substances in order to fulfill Poland's obligations towards the EU and the UNFCCC. It allows pollution to be cut in the cheapest possible way and it introduces into Polish law the three mechanisms under the Kyoto Protocol: Emissions Trading, the Clean Development Mechanism and Joint Implementation This Act sets forth the following: - Responsibilities of the National Centre for Emission Balancing and Management; - The principles of the operation of the National System for Emission Balancing and Forecasting; - The principles of the management of emissions of GHGs and other substances; - The principles of the operation of the National Registry of the Kyoto Units and emission allowance; - The principles of trading in and managing the Kyoto units; - The principles of the operation of the National Green Investment Scheme and the Climate Account; - The terms and conditions of the management of the Joint Implementation projects in the territory of the Republic of Poland; and - The terms and conditions of the management outside the territory of the Republic of Poland for the Joint Implementation projects, and the Clean Development Mechanism projects. Projects implemented in following areas can be considered for implementation under the GIS Act: (i) energy efficiency; (ii) clean coal technologies; (iii) replacement of high-emission by low-emission fuel; (iv) avoidance or reduction of GHG emissions from the transport sector; (v) use of renewable energy sources; (vi) avoidance or reduction of methane emissions through recycling and use in power generation; (vii) GHG sequestration; (viii) actions to reduce or avoid GHG emissions, to absorb CO2 and adapt to climate change; (ix) research and development of renewable energy sources and advanced and innovative environmentally-friendly technologies; (x) educational activities.

The 2025 amendment (Dz.U. 2025 poz. 1863) to the Act of 17 July 2009 on the System to Manage the Emissions of Greenhouse Gases and Other Substances introduces several updates mainly to align Polish law with EU carbon-border adjustment mechanism (CBAM) and related EU requirements, and to strengthen administrative cooperation and enforcement: the amendment adds new EU legal references (including an EU Commission implementing decision) in the Act’s reference section and updates the scope of tasks of environmental and customs authorities so that they support implementation of CBAM reporting.

Document Title: Act of 20 February 2015 on renewable energy sources (Initial text)
Family Title: Act of 20 February 2015 on renewable energy sources, last amended by Act of 9 October 2025 (RES Act)
Family Summary:
This Act defines the new regime for support to renewable energy sources (including wind energy, energy radiation of the sun, solar, aerothermal, geothermal, hydrothermal energy, hydropower, waves, currents error and tidal energy obtained from biomass, biogas, agricultural biogas and bioliquid - art. 2.22), aiming to stabilise the long-term support system, with the goal to prevent electricity prices form increasing in an uncontrolled manner. The Act also transposes the Directive 2009/28/EC on renewable energy into Polish legislation and aims to contribute to Poland's target of reaching 15% share of renewable energy production by 2020. The Act will provide continuing support to most existing installations (in operation by or before 31 December 2015) through certificates of origin, while future investments will compete for support through an auction system organised by the President of the Energy Regulatory Office (ERO). The auctions, applying to new installations over 10 kW of installed capacity, will be based on a type of contract-for-difference (CfD) granted, with separate auctions for old and new plants and for plants of 1 MW or less, as well as varied reference prices for the eighteen renewable sources reference groups. The chosen renewable plants will have to start generation within 24 months (solar plants), 72 months (wind plants) or 48 months (others), and they will be required to deliver at least 85% of the volume offered in the bid, under threat of penalty. The energy auctioned will be bought by the newly formed state-owned entity Operator Rozlicze? Energii Odnawialnej (for energy produced in installations above 500kW) or by electricity distribution companies (for energy produced in installations up to 500kW). Most existing installations will also be eligible to change to the auction support instrument from the current certificates of origin, except for hydropower plants over 5 MW, non-qualified biomass co-firing installations (art. 44) and biomass installations exceeding 50 MW of electrical power (or 150 MW of CHP power). The existing installations will continue to be eligible for the existing system of support through certificates of origin (introduced through the 1997 Energy Law), but a number of limits will apply:

  • support for max 15 years from the start of renewable energy production in the given installation;
  • support for hydropower of max 5 MW of capacity;
  • support for biomass co-firing installations of less than 150 MW capacity capped to equivalent of the average generation over 2011-2013;
  • large consumers (over 100 GWh of electricity consumed annually) and commodity brokerage houses are required to obtain certificates of origin for 14% and 15% of their annual electricity consumption for 2015 and 2016 respectively or pay the substitution fee. From 2017 the share is min 20%, but can be reduced by the Minister of Economy, if necessary due to specific market conditions. A unit substitution fee is set to a constant 300.03 PLN/MWh (2014 level, approx. 72 €/MWh), which should stabilise the price of certificates, as purchasing and redeeming of certificates of origin will only be allowed if the average market price of certificates remains below 75% of the substitution fee.

The Act also introduces a feed-in tariff system for micro installations of up to 10 kW (max 40 kW under special conditions - art. 41.7), while guaranteeing purchase by electricity distribution companies at 100% of market price for the next 15 years, until no later than December 31, 2035 (art. 41.5). The support also covers the so-called ‘prosumers,' who both produce and consume their own electricity. The subsidies amount to PLN 0.75 (EUR 0.18) per kWh to prosumers with a capacity of up to 3 kW (hydropower, wind, solar), and PLN 0.40-0.70 (EUR 0.10-0.17) for those with a capacity between 3 and 10 kW (biogas, hydropower, wind, solar). Moreover, there are no licensing/concession requirements for those micro generators that do not have active business. The Act was amended in June 2018, alongside the 2016 Act on investments in wind power and the 1994 Construction Act, in order to boost offshore wind investment. It was further amended in August 2019 to extend the agreements on grid connection and updates the auction mechanism.The Act was notably amended by bill 1129/2021 to extend the period for auctions of energy sales, set auction volumes, and remove obligations for small producers of renewable energy. The Amendments from November 2024 and October 2025 to the Renewable Energy Sources Act represent a strategic pivot toward "energy democratization," fundamentally increasing the profitability and accessibility of green energy. The November 2024 Act introduced a more favorable "net-billing" calculation and cut administrative wait times for solar building permits and grid connections from months to just 30 to 45 days. The October 2025 Act expanded these rights to urban dwellers by allowing energy cooperatives to operate in cities and granting "tenant prosumers" the flexibility to install solar panels on carports and garages rather than just roofs. Together, these laws establish renewable energy as an "overriding public interest".

Document Title: Act of March 9, 2023 amending the Act on investments in wind farms and certain other acts
Family Title: Act of 20 May 2016 on investments in wind farms, last amended by Act of 9 March 2023
Family Summary: The Act of 20 May 2016 on investments in the field of wind power plants sets out special rules for the planning, construction and location of wind farms in Poland, including spatial planning requirements and conditions for siting turbine installations. Over time this Act has been amended to liberalise wind energy development — most notably by the Act of 9 March 2023, which relaxed the stringent distance rule that previously prohibited turbines within ten times their height from residential buildings by allowing siting under local spatial plans with a minimum distance of 700 meters instead of the 10H rule, creating more opportunities for onshore wind projects.

Document Title: Act of 8 December 2017 on the capacity market (initial text)
Family Title: Act of 8 December 2017 on the capacity market, last amended by Act of 12 September 2025
Family Summary: TThe Act on the Capacity Market’s purpose is to ensure medium- and long-term security of electricity supply to end users in a manner that is cost-effective, non-discriminatory, and consistent with the principles of sustainable development.; in 2025, this framework was amended to introduce a new mechanism of supplementary (uzupełniające) capacity auctions that can be conducted after the main auctions if they fail to secure adequate capacity for future delivery periods, allowing additional contracting of capacity and helping address potential shortages under evolving market conditions.

Document Title: Act of 11 January 2018 on electromobility and alternative fuels (initial text)
Family Title: Act of 11 January 2018 on electromobility and alternative fuels, last amended by Act of 21 November 2024
Family Summary:
The Act of 11 January 2018 on Electromobility and Alternative Fuels sets out Poland’s legal framework for the development and operation of infrastructure for alternative fuels in transport — including electric charging stations and other low‑emission vehicle fuels — by defining technical requirements, obligations for public authorities to develop this infrastructure, information duties, the conditions for clean transport zones in municipalities, and the national policy framework for alternative fuel infrastructure, in order to implement key EU directives on clean transport and energy efficiency.

The Act of 21 November 2024 (Poz. 1853) amended this law to update and strengthen electromobility provisions, including: raising and clarifying targets for the share of electric vehicles in the fleets of central and local public bodies (e.g., requiring at least 22 % electric vehicles in relevant government and municipal fleets), restructuring or repealing certain older requirements, and introducing new obligations for municipalities to deploy zero‑emission or biomethane‑powered buses in public transport, as well as amendments to the rules for establishing clean transport zones (including automatic creation where air pollution thresholds are exceeded). It also updated annual reporting duties on fleet electrification

Document Title: Act of 14 December 2018 on the promotion of electricity from high-efficiency cogeneration (the CHP Act)  - Initial text
Family Title: Act of 14 December 2018 on the promotion of electricity from high-efficiency cogeneration, last amended by Act of 27 November 2024 (CHP Act)
Family Summary:
This act is centred around the promotion of electricity from high-efficiency cogeneration through a support scheme in the form of cogeneration bonus (CHP auction) for electricity generators from high-efficiency cogeneration.

This subsidy scheme is approved by the Polish Ministry of Climate and Environment, the Consultative Council of the Modernization Fund and the European Investment Bank. It totals an allocation of PLN 1.5 Billion aimed at co/financing the development of cogeneration with municipal biogas in the form of grant or loans covering 50% or 100% of eligible costs respectively. It supports the construction of new installations, or the extension or modernization of existing installations, for the fermentation of municipal organic waste and the use of the obtained biogas to generate energy through high-efficiency cogeneration.

Applications from companies are open from February to June in 2024, and are covering costs from September 2022 up to December 2030.The Act of 27 November 2024 updated provisions relevant to cogeneration in the context of industrial customer eligibility for renewables‑related entitlements and compliance obligations — notably by aligning the conditions for industrial consumers to benefit from exemptions or support under the cogeneration law with new renewable energy criteria, such as demonstrating reductions in greenhouse gas emissions or improvements in energy efficiency, and requiring updated attestations and documentation for eligibility in 2025. These changes reflect the integration of high‑efficiency cogeneration support with broader renewable energy and energy‑intensive consumer rules established in the amended Renewable Energy Sources Act.

Document Title: Amendment to the Act on Personal Income Tax and the Act on Corporate Income Tax
Family Title: Act amending the Personal Income Tax Act and the Corporate Income Tax Act
Family Summary: This document amends the Personal Income Tax Act and the Corporate Income Tax Act in order to introduce an income tax exemption for subsidies on low-emission vehicles.The Act of 26 July 1991 on personal income tax is notably amended so that in art. 21 in paragraph 1 after item 129a, item 129b is added: "129b) benefits, in particular subsidies and amounts of canceled loans, received from the Low-Emission Transport Fund;".The Act of February 15, 1992 on corporate income tax' Art. 17.1.60 is added: "benefits, in particular subsidies and amounts of canceled loans, received from the Low-Emission Transport Fund."

Document Title: Act of 17 December 2020 on the promotion of electricity production in marine wind farms (initial text)
Family Title: Act of 17 December 2020 on the promotion of electricity production in marine wind farms, last amended by Act of 9 October 2025 (Offshore Wind Act)
Family Summary: The Act of 17 December 2020 on promoting electricity generation in offshore wind farms aims to boost the production of renewable energy from wind in the Baltic sea, in accordance with EU regulations.The Amendment brought by the Act of 9 October 2025 aims to "unblock" the second phase of Polish offshore development. By removing rigid administrative bottlenecks (like the 50m placement rule) and adding the intervention auction fail-safe, the government aimed to ensure that Poland meets its target of 5.9 GW by 2030 and 18 GW by 2040.

Document Title: Amendment to Law No. 5686 on Geothermal Resources and Mineral Waters (Sustainable Exploration Framework)
Family Title: Act No. 5686 on Geothermal Resources And Mineral Waters
Family Summary: In order to increase the production and consumption of geothermal energy 10-fold, the Act was passed as a specific act to this renewable source in addition to the Act on Renewable Energy (see flagship legislation). The Act outlines the procedure, elements and sanctions for issuing transferring and revoking operation licences, auditing the actions of firms while safeguarding resource and the environment. The Act also regulates natural gas that is produced during the process of geothermal production. The Act sets forth the rules and principles for rightful ownership of the resources, during the exploration and operational periods of the geothermal and natural mineral water resources that are/will be specified along with gases with geothermal origins, their turnover, abandonment, bid tendering and terminating the use of resources and protection and extraction of resources.

Document Title: Act No. 56/2001 Coll. on the Conditions of Operation of Vehicles on Roads and other provisions
Family Title: Act No. 56/2001 Coll. on the Conditions of Operation of Vehicles on Roads and other provisions
Family Summary: Act No. 56/2001 Coll. indirectly contributes to CO₂ reduction by requiring that all vehicles meet EU emissions and technical standards before registration, ensuring that only compliant, lower-emission vehicles are operated on Czech roads.

Document Title: Act 239/2000 Coll. - Act on the Integrated Rescue System
Family Title: Act 239/2000 Coll. - Act on the Integrated Rescue System
Family Summary:
This law establishes an integrated system of emergency response and preparedness in Czechia. It requires local governments to receive and process disaster reports, issue alerts, and mobilize emergency response resources. It also requires line ministries to keep records of sector-specific risks and promote disaster risk management in their sectors. It designates the Ministry of the Interior as the lead organization for coordinating national preparedness and response efforts, and to oversee the implementation of the act.

The law was amended 17 times.

Document Title: Act No. 458/2000 Coll., on the Conditions for operating business and on performance of state administration in energy sectors (Energy Act)
Family Title: Act No. 458/2000 Coll., on the Conditions for operating business and on performance of state administration in energy sectors (Energy Act)
Family Summary:
The Energy Act (458/2000 Sb.) sets out the legal and regulatory framework for the entire energy market in the Czech Republic — electricity, gas and heat — while embedding clear provisions to support and promote renewable energy. It enables preferential grid connection for renewables, allows small‑scale and community‑based renewable generation, and provides for financial incentives, thereby facilitating the transition toward cleaner, decentralized and more sustainable energy systems.

The law was amended 48 times.

Document Title: Forest Act 289/1995 Coll.
Family Title: Forest Act 289/1995 Coll.
Family Summary:
The purpose of this Act is to establish the prerequisites for the preservation, care and restoration of forests as a national asset, forming an irreplaceable component of the environment, for the fulfillment of all their functions and for the support of sustainable management thereof.

The most important category of the Czech LULUCF sector in terms of greenhouse gas emission balance is forest land. Forestry in the Czech Republic is primarily regulated by the Forest Act 289/1995 Coll. This instrument does not specifically target carbon balance but its provisions affect carbon budget and greenhouse gas emissions and removals indirectly in a number of ways. This act was amended 32 times.

Document Title: Act 383/2012 Coll. on conditions for trading of emission allowances
Family Title: Act 383/2012 Coll. on conditions for trading of emission allowances, last amended by Act No. 481/2024 Coll.
Family Summary: The EU ETS is one of the most important economic tools to reduce GHG emissions. The scheme for GHG emission allowance trading within the Community is established in the Directive 2003/87/EC, as amended. This legislation is transposed into the Czech legal system by Act 383/2012 Coll. on conditions for trading of emission allowances, as amended. This Act defines what facilities are subject to the system and the rights and obligations of operators. Operators monitor their emissions, report to the Ministry of the Environment and receive allowances. Part of the allowances is allocated free of charge; the remainder may be bought at the marketplace or in auctions. Allowances exist and can be transferred between allowance accounts within the registry, which is administered by the Czech electricity and gas market operator OTE, a.s.

Document Title: Act 73/2012 Coll., on ozone depleting substances and fluorinated greenhouse gases
Family Title: Act 73/2012 Coll., on ozone depleting substances and fluorinated greenhouse gases, last amended by Act 218/2025
Family Summary: This Act regulates the rights and obligations of persons and competence of administrative bodies in the field of ozone layer protection and climate system protection against negative effects of regulated substances and fluorinated greenhouse gases. With regard to ozone layer protection, the Act implements Regulation (EC) 1005/2009 on substances that deplete the ozone layer, as amended, and Regulation (EU) 517/2014 on fluorinated greenhouse gases (later replaced by Regulation (EU) 2024/573). A 2023 amendment of this act banned the use of single-use containers with F-gases and established a robust certification and evaluation procedure for handling and disposing of these substances.

Document Title: Geological Storage of Carbon Dioxide Act (GSCDA)
Family Title: Geological Storage of Carbon Dioxide Act (GSCDA), last amended in 2023
Family Summary: This Act regulates public relations related to the storage of carbon dioxide in a suitable geological formation in the subsoil. The purpose of the law is the complete and permanent retention of carbon dioxide in storage sites in such a way as to prevent or eliminate negative consequences for the environment and human health.

Document Title: Excise Duties and Tax Warehouses Act
Family Title: Excise Duties and Tax Warehouses Act
Family Summary: This Act governs excise duty taxation in Bulgaria and establishes the control regime for the production, use, storage, movement and securing of goods subject to excise tax. It sets out the rules for how excise duty is charged on products such as petrol, diesel, tobacco, alcohol, and other excisable goods, and regulates the tax warehouse system under which excisable products can be stored under suspension of duty. The law also includes provisions that support energy and environmental objectives, such as reduced excise duty rates for fuels blended with bioethanol or biodiesel (e.g., a lower rate for petrol with 4–5 % bioethanol and for diesel with 4–5 % biodiesel) to promote the use of biofuels. Additionally, Bulgaria has introduced temporary zero‑excise measures for certain energy products (including electricity and natural gas) as part of fiscal policy responses, although these measures were in place up to mid‑2025 .

Document Title: Act 76/2002 Coll., on Integrated Prevention Act
Family Title: Act 76/2002 Coll., on Integrated Prevention Act
Family Summary:
Integrated pollution prevention and control (IPPC) refers to the minimising of pollution from various industrial sources throughout the EU. The Integrated Prevention Act, transposes EU legislation. The Act requires industrial and agricultural activities with a high pollution potential to obtain a permit; this permit can only be issued if certain environmental conditions are met so that the companies themselves bear responsibility for preventing and reducing any pollution they may cause.

The IPPC Directive is based on several principles, namely an integrated approach, best available techniques (BAT) flexibility and public participation.

In the area of GHG emissions, which are generated by the production and use of heat and electricity, the Act allows the regulator to apply the BAT concept, which should lead to increased energy efficiency of production. This Act also allows the application of emission limits or equivalent technical parameters, which are based on advanced technologies used in affected industrial sectors.

The act has been amended frequently since then (18 times), with the last amendment issued on 01.01.2024.

Document Title: Act 453/2016 Coll. - Consumption Tax
Family Title: Act 453/2016 Coll. - Consumption Tax
Family Summary: The Act on Consumption Tax 453/2016 Coll. amends the Excise Tax Act 353/2003 Coll. supporting alternative fuels with lower CO2 emissions by adding a provision (§ 57) that allows certain users — namely persons engaged in agricultural production or forestry/forest-management — to claim a refund (partial rebate) of the excise duty paid on mineral oils that they purchase (or produce) and use for agricultural or forestry operations. The refund mechanism is conditional: the oil must be actually used in eligible activities (crop growing, livestock production, fish farming or forestry), and the user must document the purchase and the actual consumption. The refunded amount depends on the type of activity (e.g., crop growing vs livestock vs forestry) and the volume of fuel used.

Document Title: Water Protection Act (GSchG) (Initial Version)
Family Title: Water Protection Act (GSchG)
Family Summary: The Act is designed to protect the aquatic environment from harmful impacts and to promote its sustainable use, ensuring that water management is approached holistically and coordinated at the international level. Its main objectives include safeguarding the health of humans, animals, and plants; preserving groundwater and spring water in their natural state for drinking and utility purposes; conserving and restoring natural habitats around water bodies for native flora and fauna; and maintaining water as an important element of the landscape. Additionally, the Act implements Directive 2000/60/EC, establishing a framework for water policy at the European level. It comprises 52 articles.

Family Title: Environmental Protection Act (USG)
Family Summary: The law aims to protect humans, animals, plants, and their habitats from harmful or disruptive impacts and to safeguard the natural foundations of life, including biodiversity, soil fertility, and the quality of water and air. The Act also implements relevant EEA legislation, covering areas such as packaging and waste management, landfills, fuel and CO₂ information for vehicles, air and noise pollution, environmental liability, industrial emissions, carbon storage, and the control of major-accident hazards and electronic waste.

Document Title: Energy Efficiency Act (Current version)
Family Title: Energy Efficiency Act (EEG)
Family Summary: The Energy Efficiency Act establishes the legal framework to promote efficient and environmentally friendly energy use and supply in Liechtenstein. It regulates the allocation of subsidies for energy efficiency measures and renewable energy projects, including the refinancing of loans under the EEG. The law also governs the purchase and remuneration of electricity from renewable and combined heat‑and‑power (CHP) plants and allows for the imposition of a levy on electricity consumption. Additionally, it sets rules for the management and use of state and municipal energy registers and implements Directive 2004/8/EC of the European Parliament on promoting CHP in the internal energy market.

Document Title: Emissions Trading Act (Initial version)
Family Title: Emissions Trading Act (EHG)
Family Summary:
This law indends to reduce greenhouse gas emissions within and outside Liechtenstein, and implement the EEA legislation on emissions trading. The targets are similar to EU commitments. The document details permitting rules. Art. 4 states that the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions is primarily pursued through domestic measures, in particular through energy, transport, environmental, forestry, agricultural, economic and financial measures. The share of greenhouse gases that can not be reduced by domestic measures to achieve the reduction targets under para. 1 is covered by participation in project measures abroad or in international emissions trading.

The Emission Trading Act anchors in law a GHG emission reduction target, reducing GHG emissions by 55% compared to the base year 1990. Article 4 outlines a long-term goal to reach climate neutrality by 2050, and an interim target to reduce greenhouse gas emissions by 45% below 1990 levels by 2030. Reductions are primarily to be achieved domestically, with at least 40% reduced in Liechtenstein. Liechtenstein is also participating in the European Economic Area (EEA) and is obliged to adopt EU legislation considered relevant to the EEA, which is the case for all acts under the EU's Fit for 55 package, and the current version reflects changes in EEA/EU ETS law and updated climate commitments, ensuring the law stays consistent with international obligations and linked registries and market mechanisms.

Document Title: Emissions Trading Act (Current version)
Family Title: Emissions Trading Act (EHG)
Family Summary:
This law indends to reduce greenhouse gas emissions within and outside Liechtenstein, and implement the EEA legislation on emissions trading. The targets are similar to EU commitments. The document details permitting rules. Art. 4 states that the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions is primarily pursued through domestic measures, in particular through energy, transport, environmental, forestry, agricultural, economic and financial measures. The share of greenhouse gases that can not be reduced by domestic measures to achieve the reduction targets under para. 1 is covered by participation in project measures abroad or in international emissions trading.

The Emission Trading Act anchors in law a GHG emission reduction target, reducing GHG emissions by 55% compared to the base year 1990. Article 4 outlines a long-term goal to reach climate neutrality by 2050, and an interim target to reduce greenhouse gas emissions by 45% below 1990 levels by 2030. Reductions are primarily to be achieved domestically, with at least 40% reduced in Liechtenstein. Liechtenstein is also participating in the European Economic Area (EEA) and is obliged to adopt EU legislation considered relevant to the EEA, which is the case for all acts under the EU's Fit for 55 package, and the current version reflects changes in EEA/EU ETS law and updated climate commitments, ensuring the law stays consistent with international obligations and linked registries and market mechanisms.

Document Title: CO2 Act (Current version)
Family Title: Law on the Reduction of CO2 Emissions (CO2 Act)
Family Summary: The CO₂ Act (CO2-Gesetz) is Liechtenstein’s core national law for reducing greenhouse gas emissions, particularly carbon dioxide. Its primary purpose is to implement the treaty on environmental levies with Switzerland, reduce CO₂ emissions, limit other harmful environmental impacts, and encourage energy efficiency and greater use of renewable energy. The law establishes a CO₂ levy on the production and import of fossil fuels, defines obligations for emission reductions, and sets up mechanisms for companies to commit to long-term emission reductions in exchange for possible reimbursements of the levy. It also regulates emission limits for vehicles, and lays out provisions for reporting and penalties for non-compliance. Overall, it provides a comprehensive framework to align Liechtenstein’s climate policy with international commitments and promote the transition to a lower-carbon economy.The 2025 revision of the CO2 law includes an expansion of the negotiated reduction commitments (for exemption from the CO2 levy), opening the policy and measures to all companies is considered to have an impact under the WEM/WAM scenarios. Other measures mainly affect emissions under the transport sector (article 11).

Document Title: Act No. 541/2020 Coll. on waste management, amended by Act No. 218/2025 Coll.
Family Title: Act No. 541/2020 Coll. on waste management, amended by Act No. 218/2025 Coll.
Family Summary:
The Act aims to protect human health and the environment by promoting sustainable waste management and minimizing waste generation, in line with EU rules. It establishes a hierarchy for handling waste: first preventing its creation, then preparing for reuse, followed by recycling, other recovery (including energy use), and lastly disposal.

The law requires permits for facilities that process or recycle waste, and sets strict rules for waste transportation, including cross-border movement. It also regulates composting, requiring compliance with health and environmental standards.

In terms of policy, it mandates a national waste-management plan (Plán odpadového hospodářství), developed by the Ministry together with regional authorities.

As of 1 July 2025, the Act was amended by the 218/2025 Act.

Document Title: Act No. 406/2000 Coll. on Energy Management, as amended
Family Title: Act No. 406/2000 Coll. on Energy Management
Family Summary:
The law was amended 33 times. The current wording incorporates the relevant regulations of the European Union and, in connection with the directly applicable Union regulation governing labelling requirements, provides:

a) certain measures for increasing the efficiency of energy use and the obligations of natural and legal persons in managing energy,

b) rules for the creation of the State Energy Concept, the Territorial Energy Concept and the State Program for the Promotion of Energy Savings,

c) ecodesign requirements for products related to energy consumption,

d) requirements for indicating the consumption of energy and other main resources on energy labels of energy-related products,

e) requirements for information and education in the field of energy saving and the use of renewable and secondary resources,

f) rules for implementing the principle of energy efficiency first,

g) certain rules for the provision of energy services.

The law covers a broad set of sectors: buildings (residential and non‑residential), industry, heat electricity production and distribution, heating, ventilation and air‑conditioning systems, and energy-consuming products (via requirements on energy‑labelling and “eco‑design”).
